original_abstract:
Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and Transformers have significantly advanced remote sensing change detection (CD), yet both possess fundamental limitations: CNNs are often restricted by a local receptive field that fails to capture broad spatial contexts, while Transformers suffer from high computational costs that make them difficult to scale across massive datasets. To address these challenges, we introduce the Mamba architecture—a framework based on state space models—to the field of remote sensing CD for the first time. 

By leveraging the efficiency and global modeling capabilities of the Mamba architecture, we propose a suite of specialized frameworks: MambaBCD for binary change detection, MambaSCD for semantic change detection, and MambaBDA for building damage assessment. Each model utilizes a cutting-edge Visual Mamba encoder to learn comprehensive spatial contexts from input imagery. To further refine the detection process, we introduce three spatio-temporal relationship modeling mechanisms designed to facilitate deep interaction between multi-temporal features within the Mamba-based decoder. 

Rigorous evaluations across five benchmark datasets demonstrate that our ChangeMamba frameworks consistently outperform state-of-the-art CNN and Transformer architectures without requiring high-complexity training tricks. Additionally, our results indicate that the Mamba-based design is significantly more robust to data degradation and noise. The source code is available at https://github.com/ChenHongruixuan/MambaCD.
humanized_abstract:
Remote sensing change detection (CD) is a significant application in the earth observation field. During the last few years, Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and Transformers have dominated the research in this area. However, they all have their own set of limitations. On one side, CNNs are limited by a local receptive field, not being capable of capturing global features of the image, while on the other side, Transformers are restricted by their computational cost, which are prohibitively high for large datasets. In this work, we introduce state space models, specifically the Mamba architecture, which has not been yet employed in remote sensing CD.

Based on the efficiency and global modeling ability of Mamba architecture, this work proposed three application frameworks named MambaBCD (Mamba Binary Change Detection), MambaSCD (Mamba Semantic Change Detection) and MambaBDA (Mamba Building Damage Assessment). Specifically, the MambaBCD, MambaSCD and MambaBDA models can effectively perform building change detection tasks by utilizing the advanced Visual Mamba encoder to extract the strong spatial features from remote sensing images. In addition, three spatio-temporal relationship modeling mechanisms are designed to explore the deeper interactions between the multi-temporal features within the Mamba-based decoder.

We conduct rigorous evaluations on five widely used datasets to demonstrate the merits of our proposed ChangeMamba models. Experimental results show that all our variants of ChangeMamba models significantly outperform the state-of-the-art CNN and Transformer models, while resorting to less training techniques. We also show that our proposed Mamba design is highly robust to noise and corrupted data. The code of our proposed ChangeMambaCD is available at: https://github.com/ChenHongruixuan/MambaCD.

document.output:
Remote sensing change detection (CD) is a significant application in the earth observation field. During the last few years, Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and Transformers have dominated the research in this area. However, they all have their own set of limitations. On one side, CNNs are limited by a local receptive field, not being capable of capturing global features of the image, while on the other side, Transformers are restricted by their computational cost, which are prohibitively high for large datasets. In this work, we introduce state space models, specifically the Mamba architecture, which has not been yet employed in remote sensing CD.

Based on the efficiency and global modeling ability of Mamba architecture, this work proposed three application frameworks named MambaBCD (Mamba Binary Change Detection), MambaSCD (Mamba Semantic Change Detection) and MambaBDA (Mamba Building Damage Assessment). Specifically, the MambaBCD, MambaSCD and MambaBDA models can effectively perform building change detection tasks by utilizing the advanced Visual Mamba encoder to extract the strong spatial features from remote sensing images. In addition, three spatio-temporal relationship modeling mechanisms are designed to explore the deeper interactions between the multi-temporal features within the Mamba-based decoder.

We conduct rigorous evaluations on five widely used datasets to demonstrate the merits of our proposed ChangeMamba models. Experimental results show that all our variants of ChangeMamba models significantly outperform the state-of-the-art CNN and Transformer models, while resorting to less training techniques. We also show that our proposed Mamba design is highly robust to noise and corrupted data. The code of our proposed ChangeMambaCD is available at: https://github.com/ChenHongruixuan/MambaCD.
document.input:
Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and Transformers have significantly advanced remote sensing change detection (CD), yet both possess fundamental limitations: CNNs are often restricted by a local receptive field that fails to capture broad spatial contexts, while Transformers suffer from high computational costs that make them difficult to scale across massive datasets. By leveraging the efficiency and global modeling capabilities of the Mamba architecture, we propose a suite of specialized frameworks: MambaBCD for binary change detection, MambaSCD for semantic change detection, and MambaBDA for building damage assessment. Each model utilizes a cutting-edge Visual Mamba encoder to learn comprehensive spatial contexts from input imagery. To further refine the detection process, we introduce three spatio-temporal relationship modeling mechanisms designed to facilitate deep interaction between multi-temporal features within the Mamba-based decoder. 

Rigorous evaluations across five benchmark datasets demonstrate that our ChangeMamba frameworks consistently outperform state-of-the-art CNN and Transformer architectures without requiring high-complexity training tricks. Additionally, our results indicate that the Mamba-based design is significantly more robust to data degradation and noise. The source code is available at https://github.com/ChenHongruixuan/MambaCD.
